input.prompt:
I upgraded my MSTest project to v4 and the code compiles fine. But when I run
dotnet test, some tests that used to be discovered are now failing with discovery
errors. I also noticed that my [ClassInitialize] method crashes when it tries to
access TestContext.TestName. The project compiled cleanly — are these runtime
behavioral changes in v4? How do I fix them?

input.initialPrompt:
I upgraded my MSTest project to v4 and the code compiles fine. But when I run
dotnet test, some tests that used to be discovered are now failing with discovery
errors. I also noticed that my [ClassInitialize] method crashes when it tries to
access TestContext.TestName. The project compiled cleanly — are these runtime
behavioral changes in v4? How do I fix them?

data.reasoningText: The user is asking about MSTest v4 migration issues. Let me look at their project files to understand their setup before giving advice.

arguments.description: Run tests to see actual errors

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Run tests to see actual errors

data.reasoningText:
Let me analyze the issues:

1. **Discovery errors** - In MSTest v4, there are behavioral changes. One key change is that `[ClassInitialize]` and `[ClassCleanup]` methods now require explicit `InheritanceBehavior` specification in some cases, but more importantly, the `TestContext` passed to `[ClassInitialize]` no longer has `TestName` populated (it's null) because the class initialization runs before any specific test.

2. **TestContext.TestName in ClassInitialize** - In MSTest v3, `TestContext.TestName` in `[ClassInitialize]` would return the name of the first test that triggered class initialization. 

In MSTest v4, this changed fundamentally—`TestContext` in `[ClassInitialize]` no longer has `TestName` available since class initialization happens independently of any individual test execution. The property will be null or potentially throw an exception if accessed.

MSTest v4 also introduced stricter validation during test discovery, so patterns that were previously overlooked in v3 might now cause discovery failures. Let me run the tests to see what's actually happening.

arguments.description: Run tests to see actual errors

data.content:
The error is clear. In MSTest v4, the `TestContext` passed to `[ClassInitialize]` is a **class-level** context — it no longer carries a `TestName` because class init runs before any specific test. Accessing `TestName` throws a `KeyNotFoundException`.

**Fix:** Replace `TestName` with `FullyQualifiedTestClassName` in `ClassSetup`, since at class-init time no individual test is in scope yet.

arguments.old_str:
var name = context.TestName;
        System.Console.WriteLine($"Setting up for: {name}");

data.reasoningText:
Now I can see the issue clearly. In MSTest v4, the `TestContext` passed to `[ClassInitialize]` no longer has `TestName` populated. The error is:

```
System.Collections.Generic.KeyNotFoundException: The given key 'TestName' was not present in the dictionary.
```

This is because in MSTest v4, `[ClassInitialize]` runs at the class level, before any specific test, so `TestName` is not available. In MSTest v3, the `TestContext` in `[ClassInitialize]` was actually the context of the first test that triggered initialization, so `TestName` was available.


The solution is to stop accessing `TestName` in `[ClassInitialize]` and use `FullyQualifiedTestClassName` instead, since that's available at the class level and makes more sense for class-level initialization.

arguments.old_str:

        System.Console.WriteLine($"Setting up for: {name}");

data.content:
Tests pass now. Here's what happened and why:

**Root cause:** MSTest v4 changed the `[ClassInitialize]` lifecycle. The `TestContext` passed to it is now a **class-scoped** context — properties like `TestName`, `TestDisplayName`, etc. are **not populated** because class init runs once *before* any individual test. In v3, it piggy-backed on the first test's context, so `TestName` happened to work.

**The fix:** Replaced `context.TestName` with `context.FullyQualifiedTestClassName`, which *is* available at class-init time.

**General v4 migration tip:** Any `[ClassInitialize]` / `[ClassCleanup]` code that accesses test-level `TestContext` properties (`TestName`, `TestDisplayName`, `TestResultsDirectory`, etc.) will throw at runtime even though it compiles fine. Move that logic into `[TestInitialize]` or use class-level properties like `FullyQualifiedTestClassName`.
